title: GoHighLevel vs HubSpot 2026: Which CRM Wins (Honest Comparison)
desc: GoHighLevel vs HubSpot in 2026: an honest, side-by-side comparison of pricing, features, white-label resale, and who each CRM is really for. See which one wins for agencies and in-house teams.
keywords: gohighlevel vs hubspot, gohighlevel vs hubspot 2026, hubspot vs gohighlevel, gohighlevel or hubspot, gohighlevel vs hubspot for agencies, is gohighlevel better than hubspot, gohighlevel hubspot comparison
cta: Want GoHighLevel set up and white-labeled for your agency?
body:
GoHighLevel vs HubSpot: the short answer

GoHighLevel and HubSpot both call themselves CRM platforms, but they are built for different people. GoHighLevel is an all-in-one platform built for agencies and small businesses that want a CRM, funnels, email, SMS, booking, and automation under one flat monthly price, with the option to white-label the whole thing and resell it as your own software. HubSpot is a polished, best-in-class CRM and marketing platform built for in-house teams that want deep reporting and a huge integration ecosystem, and are willing to pay per seat and per contact for that polish.

Here is the honest verdict before the detail:

  • Choose GoHighLevel if you run an agency, resell marketing services, or want every tool in one place at a predictable cost. GoHighLevel pricing does not rise as you add clients or contacts, and the top plan lets you rebrand and resell the platform.
  • Choose HubSpot if you are an in-house sales or marketing team that values a clean interface, advanced reporting, and 1,500-plus native integrations more than flat pricing or white-label resale.

The two rarely fit the same buyer, which is why the "which is better" question almost always comes down to whether you are an agency or an in-house team.

Start the 14-day GoHighLevel free trial

GoHighLevel vs HubSpot at a glance (2026)

FactorGoHighLevelHubSpot

Pricing compared: flat agency pricing vs per-seat and per-contact

This is where the two platforms feel most different, and for most buyers it decides the whole comparison.

GoHighLevel uses flat pricing. There are three plans in 2026: Starter at $97/mo (3 sub-accounts), Unlimited at $297/mo (unlimited sub-accounts), and Agency Pro at $497/mo (which adds SaaS Mode for white-label reselling). Annual billing knocks roughly 17 percent off, so the plans work out near $970, $2,970, and $4,970 per year. On top of the plan you pay usage-based costs for phone numbers, SMS, email sends, and premium AI, because the telephony runs on Twilio. The key point is that the plan price does not go up when you add clients, contacts, or users. A busy agency and a solo owner pay the same $297.

HubSpot charges per seat and per marketing contact. There is a genuinely useful free CRM, then Sales Hub Starter at $15/seat/mo. The tier most teams actually need, Sales Hub Professional, is $90/seat/mo on annual billing (about $100 monthly) plus a one-time $1,500 onboarding fee. Marketing Hub Professional is $890/mo billed annually for 2,000 contacts and 3 seats, with a mandatory $3,000 onboarding fee in year one and extra seats at about $45/mo. Because marketing pricing is tied to contact count, the bill climbs as your list grows.

A quick worked example. Say you serve ten clients, each needing a couple of users. On GoHighLevel Unlimited that is $297/mo flat, covering all ten sub-accounts. Replicating that on HubSpot, across per-seat and per-contact pricing for ten separate clients, runs into thousands of dollars a month. For agencies the math is not close. For a single in-house team of a few people, the gap narrows and HubSpot's polish starts to earn its price. See our full GoHighLevel pricing guide for the usage-cost detail.

See GoHighLevel plans and start a free trial

Features: where each platform actually wins

GoHighLevel wins on breadth. In one login you get a CRM, funnel and website builder, email marketing, two-way SMS, a phone dialer, calendars and booking, workflow automation, reputation management, and courses or memberships, plus a growing set of AI tools. For most agencies and small businesses, GoHighLevel replaces five or six separate subscriptions, which is where the cost savings come from.

HubSpot wins on depth and polish. It has the cleanest interface in the category, best-in-class reporting and dashboards, mature marketing and content tools, and an App Marketplace with well over 1,500 native integrations. If your work depends on advanced attribution, custom reporting, or plugging into a large existing software stack, HubSpot does each of those jobs more thoroughly than GoHighLevel does.

The honest trade-off: GoHighLevel's individual modules are good enough rather than best-in-class, but they are all included. HubSpot's modules go deeper, but you pay for each hub and each seat, and the most advanced features often sit behind the Enterprise tier.

The agency question: white-label and reselling

If you run an agency, this section decides it. GoHighLevel's $497 Agency Pro plan includes SaaS Mode, which lets you rebrand the entire platform as your own software, set your own prices, automatically provision a sub-account when a client signs up, and rebill usage with a markup. That turns your software from a cost line into a profit center and gives your agency a recurring-revenue product with your name on it.

HubSpot does not offer anything equivalent. You cannot white-label or resell HubSpot's software, and its Solutions Partner program is designed for referrals and for selling your own services, not for rebranding HubSpot as your product. So if your goal is to launch a branded SaaS or rebill clients for the software itself, GoHighLevel is the only real option of the two. This is exactly the kind of build our team handles through our GoHighLevel services, and you can hire a GoHighLevel developer to configure SaaS Mode properly. If you want to see how GoHighLevel stacks up against more than just HubSpot, our GoHighLevel alternatives guide and our best CRM for agencies roundup compare the wider field.

Ease of use, support, and deliverability

Neither platform is perfect, and being honest about the weaknesses matters more than the feature lists.

GoHighLevel's downsides are a real learning curve and interface complexity. Most users need two to four weeks before it clicks and two to three months before they feel fluent, simply because it does so much. Email deliverability is the platform's most-cited weakness across G2, Reddit, and the community groups, so you need a dedicated sending domain and proper warm-up. Support can be hit or miss, and usage costs for SMS and voice add up. GoHighLevel holds a G2 rating around 4.2/5, with 4.6/5 on both Capterra and Trustpilot across 5,200-plus reviews.

HubSpot's downsides are almost entirely about cost. It is easier to learn, well supported, and backed by an excellent academy, but the price escalates quickly as you add seats and contacts, onboarding fees apply on the Professional tiers, and some features you may expect are gated behind Enterprise. The interface polish is genuine, but you are paying for it.

Who should choose GoHighLevel

GoHighLevel is the stronger pick if you are:

  • An agency or freelancer that resells marketing services and wants unlimited client sub-accounts at a flat cost.
  • A small or medium business that wants CRM, funnels, email, SMS, booking, and automation in one place instead of stitching tools together.
  • Anyone who wants to rebill clients for software or launch a branded SaaS product using the $497 SaaS Mode.
  • A budget-conscious team replacing five or six separate subscriptions with one predictable bill.

Try GoHighLevel free for 14 days

Who should choose HubSpot

HubSpot is the stronger pick if you are:

  • An in-house sales or marketing team that values a clean, easy interface and wants people productive quickly.
  • A company that needs advanced reporting, attribution, and dashboards, or a large existing stack that has to integrate cleanly.
  • A team happy to start on the free CRM and grow into paid hubs over time.
  • An organization that prioritizes ecosystem, support, and polish over flat pricing or white-label resale.

If deep automation is the real goal on either platform, that is where our AI automation agency work fits, because the software is only as good as the workflows you build on top of it.

The verdict

GoHighLevel and HubSpot rarely compete for the same buyer, so the right answer depends on who you are. For agencies, resellers, and all-in-one small businesses, GoHighLevel wins on flat pricing, breadth, and the white-label SaaS resale that HubSpot simply does not offer. For in-house sales and marketing teams that want polish, reporting depth, and a vast integration ecosystem, HubSpot earns its higher price. If you are an agency weighing the two, the economics point clearly to GoHighLevel, and the only real question is whether you are ready for the learning curve that comes with all that breadth.
faqs: Is GoHighLevel or HubSpot better in 2026?

It depends on who you are. GoHighLevel is better for agencies and small businesses that want an all-in-one platform at flat pricing, plus the option to white-label and resell it. HubSpot is better for in-house sales and marketing teams that want a polished interface, advanced reporting, and a large integration ecosystem, and can absorb per-seat and per-contact pricing.

Usually yes, especially at scale. GoHighLevel is flat, from $97 to $497 a month, no matter how many clients, contacts, or users you add. HubSpot charges per seat and per marketing contact, plus onboarding fees on the Professional tiers, so the bill grows as your team and list grow.
Can you white-label or resell HubSpot like GoHighLevel?

No. HubSpot does not let you rebrand or resell its software, and its Solutions Partner program is for referrals and selling your own services. GoHighLevel's $497 Agency Pro plan includes SaaS Mode, which lets you rebrand the platform, set your own prices, and rebill clients for it.

For many agencies and small businesses, yes, because GoHighLevel bundles CRM, funnels, email, SMS, booking, and automation in one tool. But HubSpot offers deeper reporting and a much larger native integration ecosystem, so teams that depend on those may still prefer it.
Is GoHighLevel harder to learn than HubSpot?

Yes. GoHighLevel has a steeper learning curve, typically two to four weeks before it clicks, because it packs so many tools into one platform. HubSpot's interface is more polished and beginner-friendly, and its academy makes onboarding easier.
Which is better for a marketing agency, GoHighLevel or HubSpot?

GoHighLevel, in most cases. Its unlimited sub-accounts, flat pricing, and white-label SaaS resale are built for agencies, and none of those exist in HubSpot. HubSpot can still suit an agency's own in-house marketing, but not client resale.
